notes: Integrated mobile Pentium M + chipset + 802.11 wireless; $300M marketing launch March 12 2003; over 5hr battery life; succeeded by Intel Evo branding
802.11b dominant at 11 Mbps in 2003; evolved through 802.11g/n/ac/ax/be (Wi-Fi 6E/7); now ubiquitous worldwide
Proprietary wireless authentication protocol being opened to industry in 2003; found vulnerable to dictionary attacks by 2004; replaced by EAP-FAST and WPA2-Enterprise
Server OS with wireless hardening capabilities; Microsoft proved wireless security on own campus; reached end of life July 2015
Mobile processor component of Centrino platform; built for long battery life and performance vs existing mobile P3/P4; succeeded by Core series
Unauthorized consumer APs ($90 at CompUSA) plugged into corporate RJ45 jacks; 150ft exposure radius; still a recognized threat mitigated by WIDS/WIPS
Intel invested tens of millions to accelerate worldwide hotspot network; evolved from paid hotspots to predominantly free Wi-Fi by 2010s
